sections.content: Let me check the current state after moving DOWN.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'c', 'W', 'G', 'b']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"id={n['id']}, color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, bbox=({min(rows)},{min(cols)})-({max(rows)},{max(cols)}), children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The left square changed color:
- Before: id=3, color=c (charcoal), bbox=(24,20)-(27,23)
- After: id=3, color=G (dark gray), bbox=(24,20)-(27,23)
The frame changed from charcoal (c) to dark gray (G)! The blue center (id=5) is still there.
Also, the white strip is now at (23,20)-(23,23), which is a horizontal strip at row 23, cols 20-23. The player is at (20,20)-(22,23).
So when I moved DOWN, the white strip moved down to row 23, and the left square's frame changed from c to G.
This suggests that when the white strip touches the square's frame, the frame color changes. This is the collection mechanic!
Let me continue moving DOWN to fully collect the square.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The left square frame changed from c to G when the white strip touched it.
# Let me continue moving DOWN to fully collect it.
action(['DOWN', 'DOWN'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
